message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A majority of patients suffering from acute COVID-19 are expected to recover symptomatically and functionally. However there are reports that some people continue to experience symptoms even beyond the stage of acute infection. This phenomenon has been called longcovid.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Study design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This study attempted to analyse symptoms reported by users on twitter self-identifying as longcovid.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The search was carried out using the twitter public streaming application programming interface using a relevant search term.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We could identify 89 users with usable data in the tweets posted by them. A majority of users described multiple symptoms the most common of which were fatigue, shortness of breath, pain and brainfog/concentration difficulties. The most common course of symptoms was episodic.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Given the public health importance of this issue, the study suggests that there is a need to better study post acute-COVID symptoms.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: An Analysis of Self-reported Longcovid Symptoms on Twitter

reference.article-title: Prevalence and severity of corona virus disease 2019 (COVID-19): A systematic review and meta-analysis
reference.journal-title: J Clin Virol Off Publ Pan Am Soc Clin Virol
